FAQ

Frequently asked questions about Tether. Can't find what you're looking for? Open an issue.

General

What is Tether?

Tether is a lightweight, mobile-optimized web terminal controller. It runs a local HTTP/WebSocket server on your laptop and lets you control shell sessions from your phone's browser — no cloud dependencies, fully self-hosted.

Why would I use this?

Common use cases:

  • Monitoring long-running processes from the couch
  • Quick SSH sessions without opening a laptop lid
  • Checking server logs while away from your desk
  • Running commands while cooking (yes, really)

Is this like SSH?

Similar concept, different implementation. SSH connects to an SSH daemon. Tether connects to a local PTY via a web interface. Tether is designed for mobile UX — touch-optimized, preset buttons, input-wait notifications.

Installation

What platforms are supported?

Server: Linux (primary), macOS (supported), Windows (experimental).
Client: Any modern browser (Chrome, Safari, Firefox, Edge).

Do I need Rust installed?

Only if you're building from source. Pre-built binaries are available on the Releases page.

Can I install it with cargo install?

Yes: cargo install --git https://github.com/ssnelavala-masstcs/tether.git

Usage

How do I access Tether from my phone?

  1. Start Tether with --allow-lan
  2. Note the printed IP address (e.g., http://192.168.1.100:8080)
  3. Open that URL on your phone's browser
  4. Enter the password you set

Can I use Tether over the internet?

Not directly. Tether is designed for LAN use only. For remote access, place it behind a reverse proxy (nginx, caddy) with TLS. See the Security guide for configuration examples.

How many terminals can I run?

As many as your system can handle. Each PTY uses ~5-10MB of RAM. 5 concurrent shells typically use ≤40MB total.

What shell does it use?

It uses your default shell (determined by the $SHELL environment variable). Usually bash or zsh.

Troubleshooting

"Connection refused" on my phone

Make sure:

  • You started Tether with --allow-lan
  • Your phone is on the same WiFi network
  • No firewall is blocking the port (default: 8080)
  • The IP address hasn't changed (DHCP lease)

Terminal doesn't fit the screen

The terminal should auto-resize. Try:

  • Rotating your phone to landscape
  • Refreshing the page
  • Checking that the FitAddon is loaded (check browser console)

Input doesn't produce output

Check the WebSocket connection:

  • Open browser DevTools → Network → WS
  • Verify the WebSocket connection is established
  • Check server logs for errors

QR code doesn't scan

The QR code contains the full URL including the LAN IP. If it's not scanning:

  • Make sure your terminal font size isn't too small
  • Try the printed URL instead
  • Ensure your phone's camera has good lighting

Security

Is Tether secure?

Tether uses Argon2id for password hashing, session cookies with expiry, and rate limiting. However, it does NOT use TLS — LAN traffic is unencrypted. Only use on trusted networks. See the Security guide for details.

Can someone on my network intercept my session?

On an untrusted network, yes — a MITM attacker could potentially capture traffic. This is why Tether is designed for trusted LANs only. For untrusted networks, use a reverse proxy with TLS.

What happens to my sessions when I close the browser?

The WebSocket disconnect triggers a 30-second grace period. If you don't reconnect, the PTY is killed. Your shell history is preserved (it's your normal shell, after all).

Development

How do I contribute?

Fork the repo, make your changes, and open a Pull Request. See the README for development setup instructions.

How do I report a bug?

Open an issue on GitHub with:

  • Steps to reproduce
  • Expected vs actual behavior
  • OS, Rust version, and browser info

What's the license?

MIT License. See the LICENSE file.
